Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running as a server versus when running a CLI command. However, the Flask app factory pattern is designed to create a single app instance, so you can't directly use a different `create_app` function based on the context. Instead, you can pass parameters to the `create_app` function based on the environment or the command being executed. This allows you to customize the app's configuration and behavior depending on whether it's running as a server or as a CLI command.

You can achieve this by checking the command-line arguments or environment variables. For instance, when running the CLI command, you can determine the context and pass a parameter to `create_app`. This way, the app can be configured differently based on the invocation method. While using an environment variable is a common approach, you can also use other methods to pass parameters without requiring the user to set an environment variable each time.

If you want to avoid setting an environment variable, you can use the `flask` command-line interface (CLI) to pass arguments directly to your custom CLI commands. This can be done by modifying the `@app.cli.command` decorator to accept arguments. For example, you can define a CLI command with parameters that are passed when the command is invoked. This approach allows you to pass arguments without the need for environment variables.

To pass parameters to `create_app`, you can use the `app` object that is created by the `create_app` function. When running the CLI command, you can access the app instance and modify its configuration based on the command-line arguments. This method allows you to customize the app's behavior dynamically. You can also use the `flask` CLI to pass arguments to your custom commands, which can then be used to influence the behavior of the app.

Another way to pass parameters is by using the `FLASK_APP` environment variable, which can point to a specific configuration or a custom `create_app` function. However, this approach still requires the user to set the environment variable. If you want to avoid this, you can use the `flask` CLI's ability to pass arguments to your custom commands. This can be done by defining your CLI command to accept arguments, which are then used to configure the app when it's created.
